Intro

A field guide to building AI agents that actually work

Harness &
Loop Engineering

“I don't prompt Claude anymore.
I have loops running that prompt Claude.
My job is to write loops.”

— Boris Cherny, head of Claude Code, Anthropic · June 2026

10×
Leverage over
prompting
259
PRs in 30 days
all agent-written
CC10x
Living proof
The assumption that's killing your agents

We've been thinking
about this wrong.

🧠
The old mental model
Better model → better output.

Swap in the next release. Iterate on your prompt. Hope for the best.

The model is the product.
⚙️
The right mental model
Agent = Model + Harness.

The same model, constrained and looped correctly, outperforms a "better" model running freely.

The harness is the product.

Harness design creates up to 10× leverage over prompting the same model. The shift: prompt → context → harness → loop.

The evolution — June 2026

Four layers. Each nests the one before.

Harness Engineering · 2026
Loop Engineering · June 2026
Context Engineering · 2025
Prompt Engineering · 2022
words → context → environment → cycle
Prompt
Wording: role, format, steps, examples
Context
Assembly: history, RAG, tools, CLAUDE.md
Harness
Runtime: sandbox, retries, gates, hooks
Loop
Cycle: trigger → act → verify → learn

cc10x covers harness AND loop. Most tools only cover the loop.

The elephant in the room

"Loop engineering is a psyop."

What the skeptics say
r/ClaudeCode: "I am really struggling to believe that loop engineering is the applied AI skill I should be developing right now. Seems fishy."

The Register: "Loop engineering, latest AI buzzword, still needs humans in the loop."

Hacker News: "Tokenmaxxing with extra steps. Diminishes the work product of engineers."

They're right to be skeptical.
Why they're wrong
The skepticism is about hype without proof. And that's fair — most "loop engineering" content is think-pieces, not working systems.

But the pattern is real. Reflexion (Shinn et al., 2023) proved structured reflection adds +11 points on HumanEval. Boris Cherny isn't selling a course — he's describing how his team actually works.

The proof isn't in the buzzword. It's in the system.

A buzzword without a working system is hype. A working system without the vocabulary is unlabeled. CC10x is both.

Part I — Harness Engineering

What is a harness?

The harness is everything around the LLM call —
the scaffolding that determines what the model sees,
what it's allowed to do, and how its output is verified.
👁
Context control
What the model sees. Which files. Which history. Which constraints. Curated, not dumped.
🚧
Permission surface
What the model can touch. WRITE agents vs READ-ONLY agents. Roles enforced structurally, not by asking nicely.
✅
Verification gates
Evidence before advancement. No phase exits without proof. "exit 0 or it didn't happen."
Harness Engineering — CC10x as living proof

The router owns the harness.
Agents never see each other.

cc10x-router sole entry point
→
component-builder WRITE · TDD first
→
code-reviewer READ-ONLY · 6-pass adversarial
+
failure-hunter READ-ONLY · silent failures
→
integration-verifier exit 0 required
→
phase_exit_gate fail-closed
→
memory-update task-enforced
Deterministic routing
Router matches intent to workflow by keyword signal priority. No model judgment. No ambiguity. ERROR always beats BUILD.
Tiered model policy
Planner + verifier → capable model. Builder → standard. Doc-sync → cheap. Harness determines cost, not vibes.
Fail-closed gates
No phase advances on prose alone. CONTRACT YAML required. Missing or malformed? Workflow stops. Not retries — stops.
Harness Engineering — expertise injection

Skills are constraints,
not suggestions.

## SKILL_HINTS (injected by router) ## agent never self-activates these if request targets_existing_code(): inject("cc10x:codebase-hygiene") if request targets_ui(): inject("cc10x:frontend") ## authoring + critique in one skill if human_review is_incoming(): inject("cc10x:code-review") ## verify before agreeing if request is_greenfield(): inject("cc10x:architecture") ## functionality-first design
Router is the only authority
Agents cannot self-activate domain skills. The harness decides what expertise is in scope — not the model's judgment about what it needs.
17 skills, contextually loaded
Not all loaded all the time. Lean prompt surface. Each skill is injected only when the request pattern matches — deterministic, not probabilistic.
Skill precedence is law
user instructions → project standards → approved plan → domain skills → cc10x internals → model heuristics. The harness enforces this stack every time.
A harness without feedback loops is just
a very expensive one-shot.

The harness sets the stage.
Loop engineering is what makes the agent converge.

Part II — Loop Engineering

Not retries.
Convergence loops.

✗
Not retries — hoping the model gets it right on attempt N
✗
Not polling — running the same check until it passes
✓
Evidence-driven iteration — each loop consumes findings from the prior cycle as structured input
✓
Bounded convergence — hard limits prevent infinite drift. At N cycles: human checkpoint.
✓
Memory across loops — patterns.md ## Common Gotchas — what failed once never fails the same way twice
Loop Engineering — CC10x as living proof

Three loops.
All bounded. All evidence-gated.

🔁
Remediation loop
reviewer finds issues →
router creates REMFIX task →
builder fixes →
re-review / re-verify

max 3 cycles  then: human checkpoint
🐛
Debug loop (Reflexion-style)
LOG FIRST → reproduce → hypothesize → fix

Each failed attempt is stored in patterns.md as a gotcha.

verbal memory  improves next session
📋
Plan review loop
planner writes plan →
plan-gap-reviewer anti-anchors →
REPLAN if blocking findings

fresh context  no rubber-stamping

Every loop has an exit condition based on evidence, not time or count.
The gate that triggers the loop is the same gate that closes it.

Research parallel

CC10x is Reflexion
applied to software engineering.

Concept Reflexion (Shinn et al. 2023) CC10x
Verbal memory Agents reflect on failure in natural language, stored as episodic memory patterns.md ## Common Gotchas — debug failures stored as structured notes, loaded next session
Feedback signal Task evaluator score drives reflection Reviewer YAML contract — REMEDIATION_NEEDED: true drives next loop
Convergence Loop until eval passes or trial limit Loop until phase_exit_gate passes — or human checkpoint at cycle 3
Result 91% on HumanEval vs 80% GPT-4 Quality gaps caught in BUILD are closed before merge — not discovered in prod

The paper achieved +11 points by adding structured reflection.

259
PRs in 30 days
all agent-written
CC10x
Living proof
The assumption that's killing your agents

We've been thinking
about this wrong.

🧠
The old mental model
Better model → better output.

Swap in the next release. Iterate on your prompt. Hope for the best.

The model is the product.
⚙️
The right mental model
Agent = Model + Harness.

The same model, constrained and looped correctly, outperforms a "better" model running freely.

The harness is the product.

Harness design creates up to 10× leverage over prompting the same model. The shift: prompt → context → harness → loop.

The evolution — June 2026

Four layers. Each nests the one before.

Harness Engineering · 2026
Loop Engineering · June 2026
Context Engineering · 2025
Prompt Engineering · 2022
words → context → environment → cycle
Prompt
Wording: role, format, steps, examples
Context
Assembly: history, RAG, tools, CLAUDE.md
Harness
Runtime: sandbox, retries, gates, hooks
Loop
Cycle: trigger → act → verify → learn

cc10x covers harness AND loop. Most tools only cover the loop.

The elephant in the room

"Loop engineering is a psyop."

What the skeptics say
r/ClaudeCode: "I am really struggling to believe that loop engineering is the applied AI skill I should be developing right now. Seems fishy."

The Register: "Loop engineering, latest AI buzzword, still needs humans in the loop."

Hacker News: "Tokenmaxxing with extra steps. Diminishes the work product of engineers."

They're right to be skeptical.
Why they're wrong
The skepticism is about hype without proof. And that's fair — most "loop engineering" content is think-pieces, not working systems.

But the pattern is real. Reflexion (Shinn et al., 2023) proved structured reflection adds +11 points on HumanEval. Boris Cherny isn't selling a course — he's describing how his team actually works.

The proof isn't in the buzzword. It's in the system.

A buzzword without a working system is hype. A working system without the vocabulary is unlabeled. CC10x is both.

Part I — Harness Engineering

What is a harness?

The harness is everything around the LLM call —
the scaffolding that determines what the model sees,
what it's allowed to do, and how its output is verified.
👁
Context control
What the model sees. Which files. Which history. Which constraints. Curated, not dumped.
🚧
Permission surface
What the model can touch. WRITE agents vs READ-ONLY agents. Roles enforced structurally, not by asking nicely.
✅
Verification gates
Evidence before advancement. No phase exits without proof. "exit 0 or it didn't happen."
Harness Engineering — CC10x as living proof

The router owns the harness.
Agents never see each other.

cc10x-router sole entry point
→
component-builder WRITE · TDD first
→
code-reviewer READ-ONLY · 6-pass adversarial
+
failure-hunter READ-ONLY · silent failures
→
integration-verifier exit 0 required
→
phase_exit_gate fail-closed
→
memory-update task-enforced
Deterministic routing
Router matches intent to workflow by keyword signal priority. No model judgment. No ambiguity. ERROR always beats BUILD.
Tiered model policy
Planner + verifier → capable model. Builder → standard. Doc-sync → cheap. Harness determines cost, not vibes.
Fail-closed gates
No phase advances on prose alone. CONTRACT YAML required. Missing or malformed? Workflow stops. Not retries — stops.
Harness Engineering — expertise injection

Skills are constraints,
not suggestions.

## SKILL_HINTS (injected by router) ## agent never self-activates these if request targets_existing_code(): inject("cc10x:codebase-hygiene") if request targets_ui(): inject("cc10x:frontend") ## authoring + critique in one skill if human_review is_incoming(): inject("cc10x:code-review") ## verify before agreeing if request is_greenfield(): inject("cc10x:architecture") ## functionality-first design
Router is the only authority
Agents cannot self-activate domain skills. The harness decides what expertise is in scope — not the model's judgment about what it needs.
17 skills, contextually loaded
Not all loaded all the time. Lean prompt surface. Each skill is injected only when the request pattern matches — deterministic, not probabilistic.
Skill precedence is law
user instructions → project standards → approved plan → domain skills → cc10x internals → model heuristics. The harness enforces this stack every time.
A harness without feedback loops is just
a very expensive one-shot.

The harness sets the stage.
Loop engineering is what makes the agent converge.

Part II — Loop Engineering

Not retries.
Convergence loops.

✗
Not retries — hoping the model gets it right on attempt N
✗
Not polling — running the same check until it passes
✓
Evidence-driven iteration — each loop consumes findings from the prior cycle as structured input
✓
Bounded convergence — hard limits prevent infinite drift. At N cycles: human checkpoint.
✓
Memory across loops — patterns.md ## Common Gotchas — what failed once never fails the same way twice
Loop Engineering — CC10x as living proof

Three loops.
All bounded. All evidence-gated.

🔁
Remediation loop
reviewer finds issues →
router creates REMFIX task →
builder fixes →
re-review / re-verify

max 3 cycles  then: human checkpoint
🐛
Debug loop (Reflexion-style)
LOG FIRST → reproduce → hypothesize → fix

Each failed attempt is stored in patterns.md as a gotcha.

verbal memory  improves next session
📋
Plan review loop
planner writes plan →
plan-gap-reviewer anti-anchors →
REPLAN if blocking findings

fresh context  no rubber-stamping

Every loop has an exit condition based on evidence, not time or count.
The gate that triggers the loop is the same gate that closes it.

Research parallel

CC10x is Reflexion
applied to software engineering.

Concept Reflexion (Shinn et al. 2023) CC10x
Verbal memory Agents reflect on failure in natural language, stored as episodic memory patterns.md ## Common Gotchas — debug failures stored as structured notes, loaded next session
Feedback signal Task evaluator score drives reflection Reviewer YAML contract — REMEDIATION_NEEDED: true drives next loop
Convergence Loop until eval passes or trial limit Loop until phase_exit_gate passes — or human checkpoint at cycle 3
Result 91% on HumanEval vs 80% GPT-4 Quality gaps caught in BUILD are closed before merge — not discovered in prod

The paper achieved +11 points by adding structured reflection.

text:
CC10x applies the same insight to every engineering workflow.

The full landscape — Andrew Ng's framework

Three loops. Three timescales.
One discipline.

⚡
Minute-scale loop
Agentic coding. Write code → run tests → fix failures → repeat. The tightest loop. This is what CC10x automates — BUILD → REVIEW → VERIFY → REMFIX, bounded at 3 cycles.

CC10x lives here
📅
Daily-scale loop
Build-test-ship. Ship a feature → gather feedback → learn → plan tomorrow's work. The team loop. CI/CD pipelines, PR review cycles, retrospective learnings stored in patterns.md.

CC10x feeds this
🎯
Weekly-scale loop
Product-market fit. Ship product → measure usage → adjust roadmap → re-plan. The strategic loop. The compounding knowledge in docs/solutions/ informs this loop across weeks and teams.

CC10x compounds this

Andrew Ng: "Loops are now a key part of how we get AI agents to iterate at length to build software."
— The minute-scale loop is the foundation. Without it, the daily and weekly loops have no signal to consume.

They're not separate disciplines
The harness defines the channel.
The loop fills it with signal.
Together they produce quality.
Without the harness, loops have no gates — you get infinite drift and no convergence signal.

→ Harness defines what counts as done.
Without loops, the harness is a one-shot executor — no correction, no learning, no recovery.

→ Loops close the quality gap the harness exposes.
Workflow deep-dive — BUILD

BUILD: code that's verified before it ships

routerintent: BUILD
→
component-builderWRITE · TDD
→
code-reviewer6-pass review
∥
failure-huntersilent failures
→
integration-verifierexit 0
→
phase_exit_gatefail-closed
→
doc-syncer → memorypersist
Skills loaded
Builder: building, verification, agent-common
Reviewer: code-review, verification, codebase-hygiene, agent-common
Hunter: code-review, agent-common
Verifier: verification, agent-common
Contextual: architecture, frontend, research (router-injected per request)
The loop
reviewer finds issues → router creates REMFIX → builder fixes → re-review + re-hunt (parallel) → re-verify

max 3 cycles then: human checkpoint

Complexity gradient: trivial work skips reviewer+hunter (builder → verifier → memory). Escalates to full chain on scope increase.
Why it works
Parallel review — reviewer and hunter can't bias each other. Fresh contexts, independent verdicts. Router merges.

Anti-anchoring — reviewers skip activeContext.md (the builder's self-assessment). They form their own opinion from the diff.

Contract enforcement — TDD_RED_EXIT=1, TDD_RED_REASON_KIND=behavioral. Machine-verified, not hoped.
Workflow deep-dive — PLAN

PLAN: agreement-first, anti-anchored review

routerintent: PLAN
→
explorationbrainstorm + design
→
planner3 modes: direct / execution_plan / decision_rfc
→
plan-gap-reviewerfresh context · NO memory loaded
↻
re-planif blocking findings
→
gap-review pass 2max 2 passes
→
memorypersist plan
Skills loaded
Exploration: exploration skill (design + spike modes)
Planner: planning, architecture, plan-review-gate, agent-common
Gap-reviewer: NONE — intentionally no skills, no memory. Pure fresh-context review.
Optional: Doubt-Driven Development (for decision_rfc / critical_path)
The loop
planner writes plan → gap-reviewer challenges from fresh context → if blocking findings: re-plan → re-review

max 2 review passes

If pass 1 succeeds: prune re-plan + pass 2 (unused branches deleted).
If pass 2 finds issues: stop with clarification gate.
Why it works
Anti-anchoring by design — the gap-reviewer loads ZERO memory, ZERO skills. It has never seen the planner's rationale. It reviews the plan against the codebase, not against the planner's confidence.

Agreement-first — planner returns NEEDS_CLARIFICATION on ambiguity. Never treats its own defaults as approved.

Doubt-Driven Development — for high-stakes decisions, an in-flight adversarial reviewer challenges the design BEFORE the plan is finalized.
Workflow deep-dive — DEBUG

DEBUG: evidence before any fix

routerintent: ERROR
→
bug-investigatorLOG FIRST · 10-rung feedback loop
→
code-reviewerreview the fix
→
integration-verifierregression test passes
→
memorygotcha persisted
Skills loaded
Investigator: debugging, building, verification, agent-common
Reviewer: code-review, verification, codebase-hygiene, agent-common
Contextual: research (if stuck), architecture (if prevention is architectural)

Tools: LSP (Go-to-Definition, Find References, Type Definition) for root-cause tracing — not just grep.
The loop
Feedback loop gate (MANDATORY): build repro signal BEFORE any hypothesis. No loop → fail closed.

Hypothesis loop: generate 3-5 ranked hypotheses → test → if all fail: NEEDS_EXTERNAL_RESEARCH

Fix loop: RED (failing test) → GREEN (minimal fix) → blast radius scan → verify

3 hypothesis cap then: research or BLOCKED
Why it works
No-Loop-No-Hypothesis gate — you can't guess without a repro. Prevents pattern-matching death spirals.

Causal chain gate — must explain the full chain from trigger to symptom. "Somehow X leads to Y" is a gap, not an answer. Predictions catch symptom fixes.

Debug memory — every failed hypothesis stored as [DEBUG-N] in activeContext.md. The system remembers what didn't work.
Workflow deep-dive — REVIEW + ORIENT

REVIEW: advisory only, never mutates

routerintent: REVIEW
→
code-reviewer6-pass · confidence ≥80
→
memorypersist findings
Skills loaded
Reviewer: code-review, verification, codebase-hygiene, agent-common
Contextual: frontend (if UI), architecture (if structural)

6 passes: Security → Performance → Quality → Friction → Plan Validity → Spec Compliance

Quantitative scoring: HARD/SOFT signals, min(HARD) capped by avg(SOFT)-10. Confidence ≥80 to report.
The loop
No remediation loop — REVIEW is advisory. It never creates REM-FIX tasks or mutates code.

If verdict is CHANGES_REQUESTED: router offers "Start BUILD to fix (Recommended)" — a transition, not an automatic action.

Zero-finding gate: zero findings on non-trivial change → confidence capped at 70. Must produce positive assertions with file:line evidence. No rubber-stamps.
ORIENT (read-only)
Zero agents. Zero tasks. Zero artifacts.

The router answers inline — maps modules, traces call hierarchy via LSP, explains in the project's own vocabulary.

"Help me understand this code" never falls through to BUILD and spawns a write agent. ORIENT precedes DEFAULT.

Why: orientation is the most common request. Routing it to BUILD would mean a builder modifying code you just wanted explained.
CC10x — the full system in one view

9 agents · 17 skills · 4 workflows
One coherent harness.

Router — the harness owner
Sole entry point. Owns all orchestration state. Agents cannot see each other or self-route. Workflow artifact is the single source of truth. Gates are fail-closed.
Agent chain — execution
9 agents: planner, component-builder, bug-investigator, code-reviewer, failure-hunter, integration-verifier, doc-syncer, plan-gap-reviewer, researcher. WRITE agents produce artifacts. READ-ONLY agents produce structured verdicts. Router decides next action from verdict.
Skills — expertise injection
17 skills loaded contextually by router. Building, debugging, code review, architecture, frontend, codebase hygiene, exploration, verification — expertise on demand, not always-on context.
Memory — loop persistence
activeContext.md · patterns.md · progress.md
What failed becomes a gotcha. What passed becomes a pattern. Loops improve over time.
Principles you can take home today

The 5 laws of harness + loop engineering.

1.
One entry point, one owner. The harness owns orchestration state. Agents execute; they never route.
2.
Gates are evidence, not time. A phase ends when proof exists — not when N seconds pass or the model says it's done.
3.
Loops have a ceiling. Unbounded loops drift. Hard limit + human checkpoint at N is a design constraint, not a workaround.
4.
Expertise is injected, not assumed. The harness decides what skills are in scope. Models don't self-select their constraints.
5.
Memory closes the loop across sessions. What failed once is a gotcha forever. The system gets smarter; the model doesn't have to.
Build your own

You don't need CC10x.
You need the pattern.

# The minimum viable harness def harness(request): intent = route(request) # deterministic context = load_memory() # structured skills = inject_hints(intent) # contextual for phase in intent.chain: result = run_agent( phase, context, skills ) if not gate_passes(result): return remediate(result) # loop persist_memory(result) # closes loop return result
route() — keyword signal → deterministic workflow. No model judgment at this layer.
gate_passes() — structured contract validation. YAML, not prose. Schema, not vibes.
remediate() — creates a scoped fix task with evidence. Bounded. Re-enters the loop with new context.
persist_memory() — gotchas, patterns, decisions. The loop learns even if the model doesn't.
The takeaway
Stop chasing better models.
Build a better harness.
Engineer your loops.
The humans don't go away.
They move up — from prompting to designing the loops.
Boris Cherny: "Everyone on our team codes." The engineer's job becomes loop architecture, not prompt writing.
9
Agents
17
Skills
4
Workflows
∞
Better loops

CC10x is open. The pattern is yours. The next frontier is self-harnessing — harnesses that improve themselves from their own learnings. Go build it.

Let me count the slides. The task expects 21 slides. Let me count from the HTML.

Slides:
1. SLIDE 1 — TITLE
2. SLIDE 2 — THE BROKEN MENTAL MODEL
3. SLIDE 2b — THE FOUR-LAYER EVOLUTION
4. SLIDE 2c — THE BACKLASH
5. SLIDE 3 — WHAT IS A HARNESS
6. SLIDE 4 — HARNESS: CC10x PROOF
7. SLIDE 5 — SKILL HINTS AS CONSTRAINTS
8. SLIDE 6 — TRANSITION TO LOOP
9. SLIDE 7 — WHAT IS LOOP ENGINEERING
10. SLIDE 8 — LOOP PATTERNS
11. SLIDE 9 — THE REFLEXION PARALLEL
12. SLIDE 9b — ANDREW NG'S THREE LOOPS
13. SLIDE 10 — HARNESS + LOOP TOGETHER
14. SLIDE 10a — WORKFLOW: BUILD
15. SLIDE 10b — WORKFLOW: PLAN
16. SLIDE 10c — WORKFLOW: DEBUG
17. SLIDE 10d — WORKFLOW: REVIEW
18. SLIDE 11 — THE FULL SYSTEM
19. SLIDE 12 — THE PRINCIPLES
20. SLIDE 13 — THE BLUEPRINT
21. SLIDE 14 — CLOSE

So 21 slides. Good. Slide counter says "1 / 15" hardcoded but JS computes total dynamically.

Now let me write the review.

The task expects me to evaluate the narrative flow. Let me write the findings to the output path.

Let me think through each question carefully:

1. Story arc: Hook (slide 1) → Tension (slide 2) → Four-layer evolution (2b) → Backlash (2c) → Definition (3) → Proof (4-5) → Transition (6) → Loop def (7) → Loop patterns (8) → Reflexion parallel (9) → Andrew Ng (9b) → Harness+Loop (10) → Workflow deep-dives (10a-10d) → Full system (11) → Principles (12) → Blueprint (13) → Close (14). 

The arc makes sense. The main risk of losing interest: the 4 workflow deep-dives (10a-10d) are very dense and similar in structure. After slide 10 makes the synthesis point, slides 10a-10d are essentially the same template repeated 4 times with different content. This is the data-dump risk. An audience of 10,000 may fatigue by slide 10c.

2. Transition from slide 10 to 10a-10d: Slide 10 says "harness defines channel, loop fills it with signal, together they produce quality." Then 10a dives into BUILD with no transition. There's no "now let's see how this works in practice — four workflows" framing. The speaker needs a transition slide. The section label switches from "Proof" to "Workflows" but that's only visible at top-right. A speaker transition line is needed.

3. After 10a-10d, slide 11 (full system): The audience just saw every workflow in detail. Slide 11 is "9 agents · 17 skills · 4 workflows · One coherent harness." It's largely redundant — it re-summarizes router, agent chain, skills, memory. The audience just saw all of this in the deep-dives. It adds the "one coherent harness" synthesis framing and the memory layer detail, but feels like a recap. Could be cut or merged with slide 12. However, it does serve as a "zoom out" after the detail — which has narrative value. But it's the weakest slide.

4. Closing slide 14: Big quote, humans move up card, stat row (9/17/4/∞), self-harnessing note. That's 4 elements. For a closing slide, this is too much. The big quote is the hero. The stat row repeats what slide 11 already said (9 agents, 17 skills, 4 workflows). The self-harnessing note is a forward-looking teaser that deserves its own beat. Recommend splitting: keep quote + humans-move-up card on slide 14; move stat row + self-harnessing to a "what's next" slide 15, OR cut the stat row entirely (it's redundant with slide 11) and keep self-harnessing as the final line.

5. Backlash slide 2c placement: It comes after 2b (four-layer evolution) but before slide 3 (what is a harness). At this point the audience has heard "harness + loop" as terms but doesn't yet know what a harness IS. The backlash quote "loop engineering is a psyop" — the audience can understand "loop engineering" loosely from the four-layer slide, but they haven't been given the definition. This is actually a strength: the backlash creates tension before the definition resolves it. However, the "why they're wrong" card references Reflexion and Boris Cherny — credibility appeals that are paid off later (slide 9 for Reflexion). The placement works for narrative tension but the rebuttal is slightly premature. It would work better AFTER slide 3 (harness definition) so the audience knows what's being defended. OR keep it where it is for tension but soften the rebuttal to "we'll prove this" rather than making the full case. Currently it front-loads the Reflexion evidence that gets fully presented on slide 9, creating a轻微 redundancy.

6. Andrew Ng slide 9b after Reflexion (9): Slide 9 is the Reflexion parallel (research credibility). Slide 9b is Andrew Ng's three-loop framework (industry credibility). Placing 9b after 9 makes sense: research proof → industry endorsement → then slide 10 synthesis. Moving 9b earlier (into the "what is loop engineering" section, around slide 7) would establish the broader landscape before diving into CC10x's specific loops. But that might dilute the focus. Current placement works: 7 (definition) → 8 (CC10x's 3 loops) → 9 (research backing) → 9b (industry backing) → 10 (synthesis). This is a credibility stack. Keeping it here is fine. However, 9b is somewhat redundant with slide 8 — both present "three loops." Slide 8 is CC10x's three loops (remediation, debug, plan review); 9b is Andrew Ng's three timescales (minute, daily, weekly). Different framing but the "three loops" framing twice in three slides may confuse. Recommend a clearer differentiation in the slide title.

7. Total slides: 21. The slide counter hardcoded "1 / 15" but JS computes it dynamically, so display is correct. 21 slides for 45 min = ~2.1 min/slide. 

Dense slides for 2 minutes:
- Slide 7 (loop engineering def + SVG diagram + 5 check items): the SVG is complex, 5 check items + diagram. ~3-4 min.
- Slides 10a-10d (each has chain diagram + 3 cards with dense text): each is ~3-4 min of content. 4 × 3.5 = 14 min for this section alone = 31% of the talk.
- Slide 4 (router chain + 3 cards): dense.
- Slide 9 (compare table with 4 rows): moderate.

Thin slides:
- Slide 6 (transition): just a quote + subtitle. ~30 seconds. Could be folded into slide 7's opening.
- Slide 10 (synthesis): quote + 2 cards. ~1 min. OK as a breather.

8. ONE slide to cut: Slide 11 (the full system). It's the most redundant — the audience just saw every workflow in detail (10a-10d) and the system overview was on slide 4. The "9 agents · 17 skills · 4 workflows" stat is repeated on slide 14. Cutting slide 11 tightens the arc from deep-dives straight to principles.

ONE slide missing: A "before/after" slide showing a concrete code example — the same task done with naive prompting vs. done with the harness+loop. The talk is heavy on architecture and abstract patterns but light on a tangible "see the difference" moment. A $1000 lecture needs the audience to FEEL the difference, not just understand it intellectually. A side-by-side: "Here's what Claude produces with a good prompt. Here's what CC10x produces with the harness. Same model." That would be the proof moment that makes it real.

Also missing: a "the cost of not doing this" slide — what happens when you DON'T harness. The backlash slide addresses skepticism but doesn't show failure. A failure case study (agents gone wrong, infinite loops, hallucinated code merged to prod) would raise the stakes.

Let me also note: the narrative structure has a "credibility stack" issue — Boris Cherny is quoted on slide 1, again on slide 2c, and again on slide 14. Three appearances of the same quote-source may feel over-reliant on a single authority.

Now let me write this up.

# Narrative Flow Review — keynote.html

**Reviewer focus:** story arc, transitions, redundancy, pacing, placement.
**Scope:** read-only review. No files edited.
**File inspected:** `/Users/rom.iluz/Dev/cc10x/keynote.html` (full read, 21 slides confirmed via `data-section=` grep).

---

## Slide inventory (verified)

| #  | Label    | Section              | Density |
|----|----------|----------------------|---------|
| 1  | Title    | Intro                | Medium  |
| 2  | Broken mental model | Intro     | Medium  |
| 3  | 2b Four-layer evolution | Intro   | Dense   |
| 4  | 2c Backlash | Intro              | Medium  |
| 5  | 3 What is a harness | Harness Eng | Medium  |
| 6  | 4 Router chain proof | Harness Eng | Dense   |
| 7  | 5 Skills as constraints | Harness Eng | Dense |
| 8  | 6 Transition to loop | Harness Eng | Thin    |
| 9  | 7 What is loop eng | Loop Eng       | Dense   |
| 10 | 8 Three loop patterns | Loop Eng    | Medium  |
| 11 | 9 Reflexion parallel | Loop Eng      | Medium  |
| 12 | 9b Andrew Ng three loops | Loop Eng | Medium  |
| 13 | 10 Harness + Loop together | Proof  | Thin    |
| 14 | 10a BUILD deep-dive | Workflows     | Very dense |
| 15 | 10b PLAN deep-dive | Workflows      | Very dense |
| 16 | 10c DEBUG deep-dive | Workflows     | Very dense |
| 17 | 10d REVIEW deep-dive | Workflows    | Very dense |
| 18 | 11 Full system | Proof             | Medium  |
| 19 | 12 Five laws | Proof               | Medium  |
| 20 | 13 Blueprint | Proof               | Dense   |
| 21 | 14 Close | Close                   | Dense   |

21 slides. Slide counter is hardcoded as `1 / 15` in HTML (line ~590) but the JS `updateUI()` recomputes `total` dynamically from `slides.length`, so the displayed counter is correct at runtime. No bug, but the stale hardcoded fallback is misleading if JS fails.

---

## Q1 — Does the story arc make sense?

**Arc traced:** Hook (1) → Tension (2) → Evolution (2b) → Backlash (2c) → Definition (3) → Proof (4-5) → Transition (6) → Loop def (7) → Loop patterns (8) → Research backing (9) → Industry backing (9b) → Synthesis (10) → Workflow deep-dives (10a-10d) → System recap (11) → Principles (12) → Blueprint (13) → Close (14).

**Verdict: the arc is sound and well-sequenced.** The tension-resolution structure (broken model → backlash → definition → proof) is textbook keynote craft. The harness→loop bifurcation with a transition slide (6) between them is clean.

**The one moment the audience loses interest: slides 10a→10b→10c→10d.** Four consecutive workflow deep-dives, each with the identical layout (chain diagram on top, three cards below: "Skills loaded" / "The loop" / "Why it works"). By slide 10c the audience has pattern-matched the template and stops reading the cards — they're scanning for what's different. This is the data-dump risk, and it's real. Four near-identical slides in a row is the single biggest attention-loss point in the deck.

**Mitigation options (no edit, just recommendation):** vary the visual rhythm — make 10a the "full detail" exemplar and progressively shorten 10b/10c/10d to a single differentiating card each. Or collapse the four into two slides: "write workflows (BUILD + DEBUG)" and "review workflows (PLAN + REVIEW)."

---

## Q2 — Transition from slide 10 to 10a-10d: smooth or data dump?

**It's a data dump.** Slide 10 ends with the synthesis: *"The harness defines the channel. The loop fills it with signal. Together they produce quality."* Then slide 10a opens with `Workflow deep-dive — BUILD` and immediately drops a 7-node chain diagram + three dense cards.

There is **no transition slide** between the abstract synthesis (10) and the concrete deep-dives (10a). The `section-label` in the top-right corner flips from "Proof" to "Workflows," but that's a UI chrome hint, not a narrative beat. A speaker would need to verbally bridge: *"Let's make this concrete. Four workflows, four ways the harness and loop combine."*

**Recommendation:** add a single transition slide (call it 10-intro) with the four workflow names as a 2×2 grid or horizontal strip, each with a one-line tease ("BUILD: verified before it ships / PLAN: anti-anchored review / DEBUG: evidence before fix / REVIEW: advisory only"). This frames the next four slides as a deliberate tour, not an avalanche. Cost: +1 slide (→22), but it buys back audience attention for the deep-dive section.

---

## Q3 — Is slide 11 (full system) redundant after 10a-10d?

**Largely yes.** Slide 11 ("9 agents · 17 skills · 4 workflows · One coherent harness") re-presents the router, agent chain, skills, and memory in four summary cards. The audience just saw every workflow decomposed in 10a-10d, including which skills each agent loads, the loop structure, and the "why it works" rationale. Slide 11 adds:

- The "one coherent harness" synthesis framing (narrative value).
- The memory layer detail (`activeContext.md` / `patterns.md` / `progress.md`) — though this was already shown on slides 8 and 9.
- The headline stat "9 / 17 / 4" — which is repeated verbatim on slide 14.

**Verdict:** slide 11 is the weakest slide in the deck. It's a recap masquerading as a synthesis. If the speaker wants a "zoom out after detail" beat, it should earn its place by adding a perspective the deep-dives didn't — e.g., showing how the four workflows share the SAME harness primitives (one gate mechanism, one memory store, one router), making the "one coherent harness" claim visually true rather than just stated. As-is, it's the prime cut candidate (see Q8).

---

## Q4 — Is the closing slide (14) too crowded?

**Yes.** Slide 14 currently stacks four distinct beats:

1. The big quote: *"Stop chasing better models. Build a better harness. Engineer your loops."*
2. The "humans move up" card with Boris Cherny sub-quote.
3. The stat row: 9 agents / 17 skills / 4 workflows / ∞ better loops.
4. The self-harnessing forward-looking note.

For a closing slide, this is too much. The big quote is the hero — it should own the slide. The stat row is **redundant with slide 11** (which already stated "9 agents · 17 skills · 4 workflows"). Repeating the same numbers on the final slide dilutes the quote's impact and signals "I don't trust you to remember slide 11."

**Recommendation:** split into two slides, or cut aggressively:
- **Option A (split):** Slide 14 = big quote + "humans move up" card only. Slide 15 = the self-harnessing note as a forward-looking closer ("The next frontier is self-harnessing — go build it"). Drop the stat row entirely.
- **Option B (cut):** Keep slide 14 as quote + "humans move up" card + self-harnessing one-liner. Kill the stat row.

Either way, **the stat row should go.** It's the weakest element and it's a repeat.

---

## Q5 — Is the backlash slide (2c) placed correctly?

**Placement is narratively brave but slightly premature on the rebuttal.**

The backlash (2c) comes after the four-layer evolution (2b) but before the harness definition (3). At this point, the audience has heard "harness" and "loop" as *terms* (from slide 2 and 2b) but doesn't yet know what a harness *is*. The backlash quote — *"loop engineering is a psyop"* — is about the *term*, not the *thing*, so the audience can follow the skepticism. This works: tension before resolution is good keynote craft.

**The problem is the "Why they're wrong" card.** It fires the full rebuttal — Reflexion (+11 HumanEval), Boris Cherny, "the proof is in the system" — before the audience has seen what the system IS. The Reflexion evidence gets a full dedicated slide (9) later. Citing it here, then again on slide 9, is a redundancy that deflates the reveal on slide 9.

**Recommendation:** keep 2c where it is for tension, but **soften the rebuttal to a promise, not a proof.** Replace the Reflexion citation with: *"We'll show you the proof — a working system, research backing, and the numbers. But first, let's define what we're actually defending."* This turns 2c into a setup that pays off across slides 3→9, rather than front-loading the climax.

---

## Q6 — Is the Andrew Ng slide (9b) in the right place?

**Current placement works, but has a framing collision with slide 8.**

The sequence is: 7 (loop definition) → 8 (CC10x's three loops: remediation/debug/plan-review) → 9 (Reflexion research parallel) → 9b (Andrew Ng's three loops: minute/daily/weekly) → 10 (synthesis). This is a credibility stack: definition → your loops → research backing → industry backing → synthesis. That's a solid structure.

**The collision:** both slide 8 and slide 9b are titled/framed as "three loops." Slide 8 is CC10x's three *mechanism* loops. Slide 9b is Andrew Ng's three *timescale* loops. They're different frameworks, but the audience hears "three loops" twice in three slides and may conflate them. A $1000 audience member glancing at their neighbor will wonder "wait, which three loops?"

**Recommendation:** differentiate explicitly in the slide titles. Slide 8: *"Three loops inside CC10x."* Slide 9b: *"Three timescales of iteration (Andrew Ng)."* Make it obvious these are orthogonal axes, not the same list.

**Moving 9b earlier (into the "what is loop engineering" section, around slide 7)?** Not recommended. Placing Andrew Ng before the CC10x-specific loops would make the talk feel like a literature review before showing your own work. The current order — your work first, then the authorities who validate it — is the right rhetorical sequence for a keynote that's selling a system, not a survey.

---

## Q7 — Pacing: 21 slides / 45 min = ~2.1 min per slide

**Slides too dense for 2 minutes:**

| Slide | Issue | Realistic time |
|-------|-------|----------------|
| 7 (What is loop eng) | 5 check-list items + a 380px SVG diagram with 5 nodes, 6 arrows, and sub-labels. The diagram alone needs 60-90 seconds to walk. | 3.5–4 min |
| 10a (BUILD) | 7-node chain + 3 dense cards (skills list, loop description, why-it-works with 3 sub-points). | 3.5–4 min |
| 10b (PLAN) | 8-node chain + 3 dense cards. The chain is the longest in the deck. | 3.5–4 min |
| 10c (DEBUG) | 6-node chain + 3 dense cards with bold sub-claims. | 3.5–4 min |
| 10d (REVIEW) | 3-node chain + 3 cards (one card is actually ORIENT, a second workflow shoehorned in). | 3–3.5 min |
| 4 (Router chain) | 7-node chain + 3 cards. | 3 min |
| 5 (Skills as constraints) | Code block with 4 conditionals + 3 cards. | 3 min |
| 13 (Blueprint) | Code block (8 lines) + 4 cards. | 3 min |

The four workflow deep-dives (10a-10d) alone consume **~14 minutes** — 31% of the entire talk. This is the pacing bottleneck.

**Slides too thin for 2 minutes:**

| Slide | Issue | Realistic time |
|-------|-------|----------------|
| 6 (Transition to loop) | One quote + one subtitle line. Pure breather. | 30–45 sec |
| 10 (Harness + Loop together) | One quote + two small cards. | 60–75 sec |

Both are fine as palate-cleansers, but slide 6 is almost vestigial — it could be the opening line of slide 7 and save a slide. Slide 10 earns its place as a synthesis beat before the deep-dives, but only if Q2's transition slide is added.

---

## Q8 — The ONE slide to cut, and the ONE slide that's missing

### ONE slide to cut: **Slide 11 (The Full System)**

It's the most redundant slide in the deck. The audience has just seen every workflow decomposed across 10a-10d. Slide 11 re-presents the same agents, skills, and memory in summary form — then slide 14 repeats the "9 / 17 / 4" stat anyway. Cutting slide 11 tightens the arc from deep-dives → straight to principles (12), which is the emotional pivot from "how it works" to "what you take home." The deck goes from 21 → 20 slides, and the pacing pressure on the deep-dive section eases by one slot.

### ONE slide that's missing: **A concrete before/after proof slide**

The entire deck is architecturally sound but **intellectually abstract.** There is no slide where the audience *sees* the difference. A $1000 lecture to 10,000 people needs one moment that makes the abstract tangible:

> **Same task. Same model. Two outcomes.**
> Left side: "Claude with a good prompt" — the output, the bugs, the hallucination.
> Right side: "Claude inside the CC10x harness" — the output, the gates caught, the verified result.

This is the "show, don't tell" slide. Every other slide *describes* the value; this one *demonstrates* it. Without it, the talk is a architecture deck — compelling to engineers who already believe, but not converting anyone in the "backlash" camp. It should go between slide 9b (credibility) and slide 10 (synthesis), or right after slide 4 (the first proof claim). This is the slide that turns a $200 talk into a $1000 lecture.

**Secondary missing slide:** a "cost of not doing this" failure case study. The backlash (2c) raises skepticism but never shows what happens when you DON'T harness — the infinite loop, the hallucinated merge, the agent that "said it was done." A single real failure screenshot would raise the stakes and make the gates feel necessary, not academic.

---

## Additional observations

1. **Boris Cherny appears three times** (slides 1, 2c, 14). The opening quote, the backlash rebuttal, and the closing "humans move up" card all cite the same person. For a $1000 talk, over-reliance on a single authority is a credibility risk — if an audience member is skeptical of Cherny, three references don't compound, they collapse. Add at least one more practitioner voice (Karpathy, Andrej; or a CC10x user testimonial) to diversify.

2. **Slide 10d smuggles in ORIENT** — the third card is labeled "ORIENT (read-only)" and describes a *different* workflow. The slide title says "REVIEW + ORIENT" but the eyebrow says "Workflow deep-dive — REVIEW." This is a two-workflow slide masquerading as one, and it's the densest of the four deep-dives. Either give ORIENT its own slide (it's a distinct, important concept — "zero agents, zero artifacts") or pull it out into the synthesis section.

3. **Section labels are inconsistent.** Slides 10a-10d are labeled "Workflows" but slides 10, 11, 12, 13 are labeled "Proof." The deep-dives are inside the Proof section conceptually, but the label flickers. Not a narrative problem for the audience (the label is small), but it suggests the section taxonomy was added late.

4. **The "self-harnessing" teaser on slide 14** is the most forward-looking idea in the deck — "harnesses that improve themselves from their own learnings" — and it's buried in a 16px gray subtitle under a stat row. This idea deserves more oxygen. It's the "what's next" that makes the audience feel they're hearing from a practitioner at the frontier, not just a recap of what exists.

---

## Summary scorecard

| Dimension | Rating | Notes |
|-----------|--------|-------|
| Story arc | Strong | Tension→resolution well-constructed |
| Hook | Strong | Cherny quote + 10× stat is a clean opener |
| Pacing | At risk | Deep-dive section is 31% of talk; 4 near-identical slides |
| Redundancy | Moderate | Slide 11 and slide 14 stat row; Reflexion on 2c + 9 |
| Transitions | One gap | Missing bridge between slide 10 and 10a-10d |
| Closing | Overstuffed | 4 beats on one slide; stat row is redundant |
| Proof/evidence | Abstract-only | No concrete before/after; no failure case study |
| Credibility diversity | Narrow | Single authority (Cherny) cited 3× |

**Bottom line:** The bones are good. The arc holds. The two structural fixes that would most improve this for a real audience are (1) a transition slide before the deep-dives and (2) cutting slide 11. The one addition that would elevate it from "very good architecture talk" to "$1000 lecture" is a concrete before/after proof slide showing the same model producing different outputs with and without the harness.

---

Review complete. Findings written to `/Users/rom.iluz/Dev/cc10x/.pi-subagents/artifacts/outputs/8d71510e/keynote-review-03-narrative-flow.md`.

**Headline findings:**

- **Arc is sound.** Tension→resolution structure (broken model → backlash → definition → proof) is textbook keynote craft. The harness→loop bifurcation with transition slide 6 is clean.
- **Biggest attention-loss risk: slides 10a-10d.** Four consecutive near-identical workflow deep-dives (~14 min, 31% of the talk). By 10c the audience has pattern-matched the template and stops reading. Recommend varying the visual rhythm or collapsing to 2 slides.
- **Missing transition slide between slide 10 and 10a-10d.** The jump from abstract synthesis to concrete deep-dive is a data dump with no narrative bridge.
